ontology.foodex2_description: Potato products — fried potato strips (french fries)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of French fries weighs approximately 150 g, providing around 435 kcal, and is typically served as a side dish or snack.

nutrient_source.title: French Fries — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (G00011035)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo record G00011035 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (2.33 g), trans fat (0.13 g), and vitamin C (6.67 mg) were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 11355) for 'Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated'; these three fields are flagged accordingly. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11355 was also consulted as a cross-reference for plausibility validation for other nutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; French fries / fried potato GI reference (GI ~75, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 (high) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) for deep-fried potato strips / french fries using glucose as reference. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 × 39.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 44.4, rounded to 44.3 g per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 618 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension suitability
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit French fries due to the very high sodium content (618 mg/100 g); if consumed, request preparation without added salt and limit portion to no more than 50 g, noting that even unsalted deep-fried potato contributes significant calories and saturated fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) combined with 39.5 g/100 g carbohydrate and deep-frying elevates postprandial blood glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid French fries; the high-GI potato base (GI ~75) combined with deep-frying and a high carbohydrate load (39.5 g/100 g) produces a large postprandial glucose spike, and the dietary fibre (3.9 g/100 g) is insufficient to meaningfully blunt this response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (618 mg/100 g), saturated fat (2.33 g/100 g), trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), and deep-frying-associated AGE formation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid French fries; the elevated sodium raises blood pressure, trans fat (0.13 g/100 g) adversely affects LDL/HDL ratio, saturated fat contributes to atherogenesis, and deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products that promote vascular inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; potatoes are nightshade vegetables with solanine content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid French fries; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation, and potatoes as nightshade vegetables may trigger symptom flares in solanine-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: French fries (also known as chips, potato fries, or Khoai Tây Chiên in Vietnamese) are strips of potato that have been deep-fried in vegetable oil and typically salted. They are one of the most widely consumed snack and side-dish foods globally, served in fast-food restaurants, hawker centres, and casual dining establishments across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The texture is characteristically crispy on the outside and soft or fluffy on the inside, with a savoury, salty flavour profile. Per 100 g, French fries provide approximately 290 kcal, 3.31 g protein, 13.2 g fat (including 2.33 g saturated fat and 0.13 g trans fat), 39.5 g carbohydrate, 3.9 g dietary fibre, and 618 mg sodium — a notably high sodium level. The glycemic index is approximately 75 (high), making them a high-glycaemic food that produces a rapid postprandial blood glucose rise. Clinically, French fries are unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (sodium exceeds 600 mg/100 g threshold), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI and carbohydrate load), cardiovascular disease (trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGE formation from deep-frying, nightshade solanine content). Despite containing useful potassium (538 mg/100 g) and some dietary fibre, these benefits are outweighed by the adverse nutritional profile for most chronic disease conditions. A standard serving in Singapore is approximately 150 g (1 plate), delivering around 435 kcal and a glycemic load of approximately 44 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in French fries?
Are French fries high in sodium?
What is the GI of French fries?
Can I eat French fries if I have high blood pressure?
What are French fries called in Vietnamese?
How much fat is in French fries?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and most micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00011035 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 11355) and are flagged. GI is from the University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature for deep-fried potato strips. Overall record confidence is rated high given the primary HPB source coverage of all core macros and the use of an approved secondary source for supplementary nutrients.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00011035 — potatoes contain minimal free sugars after deep-frying and the HPB record explicitly reports 0.0). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel for this specific HPB French fries record are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11355 as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (2.33 g), trans fat (0.13 g), and vitamin C (6.67 mg) were sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 11355) as the HPB record did not report these fields; these values are from an approved source. The Vietnamese name 'Khoai Tây Chiên' is the standard Vietnamese term for French fries and is consistent with the input record's parenthetical name 'Khoai Tay Chien'. This food is served in Singapore hawker and fast-food contexts and is tagged for the SG region. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT 226803007 are cited as defensible matches; FoodEx2 code A0CKP is cited as the closest defensible match for fried potato strips — reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code against the current EFSA FoodEx2 browser if precision is required.
